data.title: IIRM Holdings India Acquires Remaining Stake in IIRM Holdings PTE
data.description:
IIRM Holdings India acquired the remaining 12,260 shares, or 2.50% stake, in step-down subsidiary IIRM Holdings PTE from its wholly owned subsidiary, Sampada Business Solutions, for $12,260.

The transaction makes IIRM Holdings PTE a wholly owned unit, according to an Indian bourse filing on Tuesday.

Sampada Business Solutions purchased these shares from Promoter and Chairman Vurakaranam Ramakrishna for the same amount.

The transaction was conducted at arm's length, the rubber and plastics products manufacturer said in the filing.

data.title: Market Chatter: Acer to Hike Prices on China-Made Products in US By 10%
data.description:
Acer plans to increase prices on China-made products sold in the U.S. by 10%, following tariff hikes by the Biden administration, Focus Taiwan reported Tuesday.

The company chairman Jason Chen confirmed the move, citing cost adjustments due to the import tax, according to the publication.

The increase will affect shipments arriving after Feb. 4. Acer is also exploring alternative supply chains outside China, reportedly.

Shares gained marginally recently.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: BP Considers Selling Lubricants Business in $10 Billion Deal, Bloomberg Says, Citing Sources
data.description:
• BP is considering selling its lubricants business in an attempt to win back investor confidence and satisfy demands from activist investor Elliott Management, who has built up a nearly 5% stake in the business, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter.
  • BP's lubricant business operates under the Castrol brand and could be worth about $10 billion in a deal. The unit is among the assets that Elliott has identified for potential disposals and the divestment could be announced during its capital markets day on Feb. 26, Bloomberg reported.
  • Representatives for BP and Elliott declined to comment, the report said.

Full story: https://shorturl.at/8JRwI

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: Earnings Flash (BA.L) BAE Systems Posts FY24 Revenue GBP26.31B
UK's Annual Inflation Accelerates to 3% in January
data.description:
Britain's annual inflation rate rose to 3% in January from 2.5% in the prior month, according to data from the Office for National Statistics published Wednesday.

Analysts expected a 2.8% rate for the month.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices were 0.1% lower, compared with the previous 0.3% rise and the forecast of a 0.3% decline.

Meanwhile, the UK's annual core inflation rate was 3.7%, up from the prior 3.2% and in line with the consensus estimate. Month over month, core consumer prices fell 0.4%, against the previous 0.3% increase and the expected 0.5% decrease.

data.title: SIA Engineering Buys Back More Shares
data.description:
SIA Engineering repurchased 43,000 shares in the open market on Tuesday for SG$102,885, according to a same-day filing with the Singapore Exchange.

The engineering services company has been authorized to buy back 22.5 million shares under its current mandate. To date, it has bought back around 5.2 million shares.

data.title: UK Core Inflation Rate Highest in 9 Months
data.description:
The annual core inflation rate in the United Kingdom rose by to 3.5% in January 2025, up from 3.2% in the previous month, marking the highest reading since April 2024 and matching market forecasts.

The annual CPI services rate was notably higher (5.0% vs 4.4% in December), as did the annual CPI goods rate (1.0% vs 0.7%).

On a monthly basis, core consumer prices declined by 0.4%, shifting from a 0.3% increase in December.

data.title: Norway's TGS to Start 3D Streamer Project in August
data.description:
TGS said Wednesday that it will commence a new multi-client 3D streamer acquisition and processing project in early August.

The Norwegian energy data and intelligence company's GeoStreamer survey, which spans 1,600 square kilometers, will be conducted at the Hammerfest Basin in the Norwegian Barents Sea.

data.title: MTQ Repurchases More Shares
data.description:
MTQ bought back 82,500 shares in the open market on Tuesday for SG$22,412, according to a same-day filing with the Singapore Exchange.

The company has been mandated to repurchase about 22.5 million shares under its current mandate. To date, it has bought back 268,700 shares.

data.title: Europe Investors Might See Crisis as Opportunity — Market Talk
data.description:
0709 GMT - Europe's zippy stock market might point to hope that current travails will catalyse bolder action on the continent, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Claus Vistesen and Melanie Debono write in a note. The Stoxx Europe 600 has hit new heights this year, driven by defense stocks amid plans for a step-up in military spending. That buoyancy might seem odd given Europe's fiscal, political and trade problems, Vistesen and Debono say. But those challenges might precisely be so great that investors see drastic action as the only option in Europe's capitals, they say. "[B]etting on Europe to get its act together means betting on stronger economic and political integration, and fiscal stimulus," the economists say. "Lots of fiscal stimulus." (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0706 GMT - Japanese stocks ended lower as concerns about borrowing costs and U.S. tariffs persisted. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 0.3% to 39164.61. Auto stocks and trading houses led declines. Honda Motor shed 2.3% and Toyota Motor fell 1.7% after President Trump said he was considering tariffs of 25% or more on automobiles. Itochu dropped 2.6% and Mitsubishi Corp. declined 1.8%. The 10-year Japanese government bond yield rose 0.5 basis point to 1.435%, the highest level since November 2009. U.S. tariff and foreign policies are in focus. USD/JPY is at 151.63, compared with 152.06 as of Tuesday 5 p.m. Eastern time. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0659 GMT - The car industry is back in focus after President Trump said he was considering tariffs of 25% on autos, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals. Analysts at Nomura noted that the U.S. is the largest export market outside of the eurozone for German car manufacturers, and Trump has taken particular issue with the European automotive sector and Europeans not buying U.S. cars. "Some car manufacturers already forewarned factory closures and layoffs prior to Trump's election in November 2024, and the prospect of U.S. tariffs only worsens the situation," they write. Nomura also expects the U.S. administration to apply reciprocal tariffs sometime in April. Currently, the U.S. has a 2.5% tariff on European cars, while the EU charges U.S. imports 10%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0655 GMT - U.S. fiscal policy is core to Deutsche Bank Research's rates forecasts for this year, its strategists say in a note. They expect a modest net fiscal easing but with unusually elevated uncertainty related to both taxes and spending, they say. Currently, however, they don't yet see anything in the data that suggests federal spending has been put on a significantly different trajectory. Ten-year U.S. Treasury yields trade within their recent range, last little changed at 4.546%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0652 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi rose 1.7% to close at 2671.52, extending gains for a seventh consecutive session. Semiconductor and battery stocks led the gains. Foreign and institutional investors were net buyers. Chip stock gains on Wall Street overnight supported investor sentiment. Memory-chip makers Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix rose 3.2% and 4.0%, respectively. Samsung's planned share cancellation also kept the index-heavyweight stock higher for a second consecutive session. Electric-vehicle battery maker LG Energy Solution added 4.4%. USD/KRW settled 0.4% lower at 1,438.50 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield was up 0.1 basis point at 2.879%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0649 GMT - The improved investor assessment seen in Germany's ZEW sentiment index released Tuesday in part "reflects increased expectations of ongoing European Central Bank rate cuts," say Daiwa Capital Markets Research analysts. Rate cuts are likely to go "perhaps into accommodative territory," they say in a note. Investors' expectations for the next six months rose 15.7 points on the month in February to 26.0, the indicator's biggest increase in two years. "And with luck, that improved investor mood will translate itself into a pickup in business survey indicators, such as the Ifo indices as well as firmer demand in the real economy," they added. Money markets price in three more ECB rate cuts of 25 bps each this year, bringing the deposit rate to 2.00% by September, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0635 GMT - BlackRock goes tactically--or short-term--overweight eurozone government bonds, reinforcing its preference for them due to tariff risks, it says in a note. Meanwhile, it stays underweight long-term U.S. Treasurys, it says. Persistent deficits and inflation in the U.S. make BlackRock more positive on fixed-income assets elsewhere, notably Europe, it says. U.S. President Trump has signaled potential tariffs on Europe. Europe's reliance on the U.S. as an export destination means tariffs would hurt the eurozone's growth more than it boosts inflation, BlackRock says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0631 GMT - The Reserve Bank of New Zealand's dovish guidance indicates potential for further, faster cuts through 2025, but there is a risk of a slowdown in the pace of easing too, Nomura analysts say. RBNZ guidance on the official cash rate signals scope for cuts through 2025 if conditions evolve as projected, Yusuke Miyairi and others say. They forecast 50bp and 25bp cuts in April and May, but see room for a more hawkish RBNZ. Asked about the pace of future cuts, Gov. Orr mentioned that the bank assumes 25bp cuts in April and May, they note, adding that the OCR is now 25bp above the upper bound of the estimated neutral range. U.S. tariff uncertainty may also lead to a more cautious approach if there is a larger-than-expected adverse growth impact, they add. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0617 GMT - The Reserve Bank of New Zealand's latest rate cut could well be the last 50-basis-point reduction it makes this cycle, with the size of cuts potentially decreasing from here, UOB economist Lee Sue Ann says. The RBNZ reiterated its expectation that the economy will recover modestly this year, with annual headline inflation forecast to remain on target, she says in a note. It suggested further scope to lower the official cash rate, with the latest forward guidance signaling the average OCR falling to 3.14% by December, Lee says. "With the OCR now much closer to neutral and the economy recovering slowly, we expect a more cautious RBNZ from here." For now, UOB looks for a further 75 bps of rate cuts at a 25bp clip for the rest of the year. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0514 GMT - The Reserve Bank of New Zealand slashed interest rates and lowered its projections for the path of the official cash rate for the year at its policy meeting. However, the central bank could end up cutting even more, says Jarrod Kerr, chief economist at Kiwibank. Kerr sees risks tilted to the downside for the OCR, as unemployment is still rising and inflation is stable. If anything, the RBNZ may need to stimulate more, by putting the economy in drive and tapping the accelerator, cutting the OCR below 3% from 3.75% now, Kerr adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0449 GMT - Australia's wage growth continues to cool and the unemployment rate is hovering around the historic low of 4.0%. The data suggests that estimates of full employment held by central bank policy makers are incorrect, according to economists. The Reserve Bank of Australia delivered its first cut in official interest rates since 2020 on Tuesday, but appeared extremely hesitant when making the announcement, citing ongoing inflation risk. One reason for the concern is that the RBA puts full employment at around 4.5%. Madeline Dunk, economist at ANZ, says full employment is closer to 3.75%. If that's the case the RBA need not fret. (james.glnn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0339 GMT - Chinese president Xi's meeting with private business leaders is likely to boost confidence amid weak domestic demand and ongoing tariff disputes with the U.S., according to BofA Securities analysts in a note. The meeting, which was the first such symposium since 2018, was much needed given the fragile confidence among businesses and households, weak demand, as well as Trump's 10% additional tariffs on Chinese products, they note. The meeting highlighted a strong focus of technology, as five out of the six entrepreneurs who spoke were heads of high-tech businesses, they note. Xi's speech emphasizes the urgency of boosting business confidence and spurring technology breakthroughs as the U.S.-China technology rivalry intensifies, they add.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Pearl River Piano Ties Up With Lehai Musical Instruments to Set Up New Enterprise
data.description:
Guangzhou Pearl River Piano Group partnered with Lehai Musical Instruments to establish a benchmark enterprise in the national musical instrument industry, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange filing on Tuesday.

The parties will also join together to develop various artistic forms in entertainment, music and stage works, the Chinese piano manufacturer said in the filing.

data.title: HSBC's Unchanged Midterm Returns Guidance in Focus — Market Talk
data.description: HSBC's medium-term guidance for its return on tangible equity will likely be a focus in management's call with analysts and investors, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note after the bank's results. The assumptions underpinning the guidance will prompt questions, analysts Benjamin Toms and Pablo de la Torre Cuevas say. HSBC kept its midterm ROTE guidance unchanged in the mid-teens excluding notable items, while consensus pencils in a 14% return. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Soon Lian Forecasts Swing to Profit in 2024; Shares Tumble 17%
data.description:
Soon Lian expects to swing to profit in 2024 from a net loss a year earlier, a filing with the Singapore Exchange said on Tuesday.

The return to profit is mainly due to strong demand in the precision engineering segment.

The company will release its financial results on or before Feb. 28.

Shares of the precision engineering company slumped nearly 17% in recent trading.

UK Inflation Rate Accelerates More than Expected
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in the UK accelerated sharply to 3% in January 2025, the highest since March 2024, from 2.5% in the previous month and above forecasts of 2.8%.

The largest upward contributions came from transport, and food and non-alcoholic beverages; the largest downward contribution came from housing and household services.

data.title: Earnings Flash (CRE.L) Conduit Holdings Reports FY24 EPS $0.79
Earnings Flash (CRE.L) Conduit Holdings Reports FY24 Revenue $813.7M
Terra Metals Reported High-Grade Metals Results From Drilling Results at Western Australia Project, Shares Rise 14%
data.description:
Terra Metals said drill results defined high-grade titanium in addition to high concentrations of copper, platinum group metals, and vanadium over the full strike at its Dante copper-platinum group elements-gold-nickel project in Western Australia, according to a Wednesday Australian bourse filing.

The results were from nine reverse circulation drill holes at Reef 1 North within the project, the filing said.

More assays are pending from an additional 24 drill holes, including 11 drill holes at Reef 1, targeting further shallow high-grade mineralization.

The firm's shares rose 14% on market close on Wednesday.

data.title: Caledonia Mining Corporation Plc: Appointment of New Chief Financial Officer
data.description:
ST HELIER, Jersey, Feb. 19, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Caledonia Mining Corporation Plc ("Caledonia" or "the Company") (NYSE AMERICAN, AIM and VFEX: CMCL) announces that its Chief Financial Officer (“CFO”), Chester Goodburn, will step down from his role on 24 March 2025, following the publication of Caledonia’s financial results for the year to December 31, 2024. He will be succeeded, with immediate effect, by Ross Jerrard, subject to satisfaction of standard checks.

Mr Goodburn has served as Caledonia’s CFO since July 1, 2022, prior to which he had been Caledonia’s Group Financial Manager and Chief Information Officer. Mr Goodburn will be retained on a consultancy basis for a brief period to ensure an orderly transition.

Mr Jerrard, aged 50, brings extensive experience in financial leadership, having been the CFO of Centamin plc from April 2016 until its acquisition by AngloGold Ashanti Plc in November 2024 for a deal value of $2.5 billion. Centamin was a former FTSE-250 dual-listed (London and Canada) mining company with operations in Egypt and West Africa. Centamin’s flagship asset was the Sukari gold mine in Eqypt which is one of the world’s largest gold mines producing 450,000 ounces of gold per annum. Mr Jerrard is a member of the Institute of Chartered Accountants of Australia and New Zealand and the Institute of Chartered Accountants of Zimbabwe.

Mark Learmonth, Chief Executive Officer, said:

“Chester has made a substantial contribution to Caledonia’s growth since he joined us in 2014, the last two-and-a-half years as CFO. Along with the board and his colleagues, I thank him for his time at Caledonia and we wish him well in his future endeavours.

“I am pleased to welcome Ross to Caledonia. He brings a depth and breadth of experience which will be invaluable to Caledonia as we advance our strategy to become a mid-tier, Zimbabwe-focussed gold producer.”
